title: Responsible Legislating Act
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry.
plain_summary: The Responsible Legislating Act makes changes to many different government programs and rules. It allows people to put more money into certain retirement savings accounts and creates stronger punishments for human trafficking crimes that happen near schools. The bill gives extra money to several government departments and extends requirements for reporting information about livestock markets for one year. It also requires the government to study foreign ownership of important shipping ports and semiconductor manufacturing to make sure they do not hurt America's economic security.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry.
subjects: Department of Health and Human Services
Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Responsible Legislating Act.

sections.title: Section 101 Livestock Mandatory Reporting Extension
sections.text: This section makes two small changes to laws about reporting livestock prices. First, it updates the Agricultural Marketing Act of 1946 by changing the year 2024 to 2025. Second, it updates the Livestock Mandatory Reporting Act of 1999 by also changing 2024 to 2025. These changes extend the time period when livestock markets must report their prices and other information to help farmers and ranchers make better business decisions.

sections.title: Section 201 Education for Separating Members of the Armed Forces Regarding Registered Apprenticeships
sections.text: This section updates the education that military members receive when they leave the service. It requires that information about job opportunities includes details about apprenticeship programs. These apprenticeship programs must be registered under the National Apprenticeship Act from 1937 and approved under specific veterans benefits laws. This will help military members learn about apprenticeships as they transition to civilian jobs.

sections.title: Section 202 Websites Regarding Apprenticeship Programs
sections.text: This section requires the creation of websites to help veterans find apprenticeship programs. The Assistant Secretary of Labor for Veterans Employment and Training, working with the Department of Veterans Affairs, must create or update a public website. Veterans can search this website by job type and location to find apprenticeship programs that are registered under federal law and approved for veterans benefits. For each program, the website must show a description including any costs, contact information, whether veterans groups endorse the program, whether the program prefers to hire veterans, and what certificates or degrees people earn by completing the program. The Assistant Secretary must also update the apprenticeship.gov website to include this same information for veterans.

sections.title: Section 301 Sense of Congress
sections.text: This section states Congress believes it is important for national and homeland security that federal agencies keep the knowledge and experience of workers who get injured or become ill while working in certain jobs like law enforcement, firefighting, air traffic control, and similar positions. Congress also believes that when these workers can no longer do their original jobs and are given new government jobs, agencies should try to place them in the same agency, same location, and at similar pay levels when possible.

sections.title: Section 302 Retirement for Certain Employees
sections.text: This section creates new retirement rules for certain government workers who get injured or become ill on the job. It covers workers in jobs like law enforcement officers, firefighters, air traffic controllers, and similar positions under both the older Civil Service Retirement System and the newer Federal Employees Retirement System. If these workers become permanently unable to do their original jobs due to work-related injuries or illnesses, but can still work in other government positions, they may be able to keep some of their retirement benefits from their original job. The new job must be in an agency that regularly hires people for supervisory or management positions related to their former job. This special treatment only applies if they move to the new job within three days and file the right paperwork. The benefit stops if they get promoted to supervisory positions or reach the age when they would have had to retire from their original job. This section also covers similar workers at the Central Intelligence Agency and in the Foreign Service. Federal agencies have one year to write the detailed rules for how this will work, and the new law applies to people who get injured or become ill at least two years after the law is passed.
